meta.title: C++
meta.description: Review checklist for C++ applications and libraries (C++17/20/23)

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change and any relevant context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add context about why the allocator was changed?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves reviewer time.

items.text: Do names follow the project's style guide?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Classes, functions, variables, and constants should follow the project's established naming conventions (Google, LLVM, Qt, etc.).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming increases cognitive load when reading unfamiliar code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new names against the project style. Verify consistency within the same file.
baseContent.exampleComment: The project uses snake_case for functions but this new function uses camelCase.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Match the existing project style. Consistency matters more than any specific convention.

items.text: Are resources managed with RAII (smart pointers, scope guards)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every resource acquisition (memory, files, locks, sockets) should be tied to an object's lifetime using RAII wrappers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Manual resource management with new/delete or malloc/free is error-prone. Exceptions, early returns, or forgotten cleanup cause leaks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for raw new/delete, malloc/free. Check that resources are wrapped in smart pointers or RAII types.
baseContent.exampleComment: This allocates with new but the delete is only reached on the happy path. Use std::unique_ptr instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
auto w = std::make_unique<Widget>();
process(w.get()); // automatically cleaned up

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use raw new/delete. Use std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr, or stack allocation.

items.text: Are there no dangling pointers or references?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pointers and references must not outlive the object they refer to. Returning a reference to a local variable or using a pointer after free is undefined behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dangling pointers cause crashes, security vulnerabilities, and unpredictable behavior that may only appear in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for references to temporaries, pointers stored beyond object lifetime, and use-after-move.
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns a reference to a local string. The caller gets a dangling reference. Return by value instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never return references or pointers to local variables. Be aware of object lifetimes.

items.text: Are array and container accesses bounds-checked where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing arrays or containers out of bounds is undefined behavior. Use .at() for checked access or validate indices before use.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Out-of-bounds access causes buffer overflows, which are the most common source of security vulnerabilities in C++.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for operator[] with unvalidated indices, especially on user-provided or computed indices.
baseContent.exampleComment: This accesses vec[index] without bounds checking. Use vec.at(index) or validate index < vec.size() first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate indices before access. Use .at() when performance is not critical. Use std::span for safe array views.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objects should be moved (std::move) when transferring ownership to avoid unnecessary copies. But do not use a moved-from object.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary copies waste CPU and memory. Using moved-from objects is a common source of bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for large objects passed by value that could be moved. Look for use-after-move.
baseContent.exampleComment: This passes a large vector by value. Since the caller does not need it afterward, use std::move to avoid the copy.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Move when transferring ownership. Never use an object after moving from it.

items.text: Is const used appropriately on references, pointers, and member functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Parameters, member functions, and variables that do not modify state should be marked const.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Const communicates intent, prevents accidental modification, enables compiler optimizations, and allows use with const objects.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check parameters passed by reference for missing const. Check member functions that do not modify state for missing const qualifier.
baseContent.exampleComment: This method does not modify any member variables. Mark it const so it can be called on const instances.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to const. Only remove it when mutation is needed.

items.text: Are standard library algorithms used instead of hand-rolled loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use std::find, std::transform, std::accumulate, ranges, etc. instead of manual for loops where they express intent more clearly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: STL algorithms are well-tested, often optimized, and communicate intent (find vs a loop with a break).
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for loops that search, transform, or filter collections. Could an algorithm replace them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This loop searches for an element. std::find_if would express the intent more clearly.

codeExamples.label: Manual loop
STL algorithm
codeExamples.code:
auto it = std::find_if(vec.begin(), vec.end(),
    [&](const auto& item) { return item.name == target; });

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer algorithms over raw loops. They express what, not how.

baseContent.whatItMeans: auto should be used when the type is obvious from context (auto x = std::make_unique<Foo>()) but not when it obscures the type.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Overuse of auto can make code harder to read when the type is not obvious. Underuse leads to verbose, repetitive declarations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for auto where the type is unclear without hovering in an IDE. Also check for unnecessarily verbose explicit types.
baseContent.exampleComment: auto result = computeValue() makes the type unclear. Use explicit type here since the function name does not imply it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use auto when the type is clear from context. Spell it out when it is not.

items.text: Is std::string_view used for non-owning string parameters?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions that read but do not own strings should take std::string_view instead of const std::string& to avoid unnecessary copies from string literals.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Passing a string literal to const std::string& creates a temporary std::string allocation. string_view avoids this.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function parameters that take const std::string& but only read the value.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function only reads the string. Use std::string_view to avoid allocating when called with a literal.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use string_view for read-only string parameters. Store std::string when ownership is needed.

items.text: Are std::optional and std::variant used instead of sentinel values?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use std::optional for values that may be absent instead of returning -1, nullptr, or magic values. Use std::variant for tagged unions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sentinel values are easy to forget to check. optional and variant make the possibility of absence or alternatives explicit in the type system.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions returning -1, nullptr, or empty strings to indicate absence. These are candidates for std::optional.
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns -1 when not found. Use std::optional<int> to make the absence explicit and force callers to check.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use std::optional for maybe-absent values. Use std::variant for type-safe unions.

items.text: Is shared mutable state protected from data races?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Any data accessed from multiple threads must be protected by a mutex, atomic, or other synchronization mechanism.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data races are undefined behavior in C++. They cause crashes, corrupted data, and security vulnerabilities that are extremely hard to reproduce.
baseContent.howToVerify: Identify shared mutable state. Verify it is protected by a lock or is atomic. Check for unlocked access paths.
baseContent.exampleComment: This counter is incremented from multiple threads without synchronization. Use std::atomic<int> or protect with a mutex.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: All shared mutable state needs synchronization. Prefer std::atomic for simple counters and flags.

items.text: Are locks held for the minimum necessary duration?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lock scopes should be as small as possible. Do not hold locks during I/O, allocations, or other slow operations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Holding locks too long causes contention, reducing parallelism and throughput. It can also cause deadlocks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check lock_guard/unique_lock scopes. Is the lock held during any slow operations that do not need it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This lock is held during the entire HTTP request. Lock only around the shared data access.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Hold locks as briefly as possible. Copy data out, release the lock, then process.

items.text: Are multiple locks acquired in a consistent order?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When acquiring multiple locks, always acquire them in the same global order. Use std::scoped_lock for multiple locks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Acquiring locks in different orders across threads creates deadlocks that freeze the application.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for code that acquires multiple locks. Verify the order is consistent or std::scoped_lock is used.
baseContent.exampleComment: Thread A locks mutex1 then mutex2, but this code locks mutex2 then mutex1. Use std::scoped_lock(mutex1, mutex2) to avoid deadlock.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use std::scoped_lock for multiple locks. It handles ordering and exception safety.

items.text: Are tests included for new or changed behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features and bug fixes should include tests. Unit tests with Google Test, Catch2, or the project's test framework.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without tests, regressions go undetected. C++ bugs can be subtle and hard to reproduce without automated verification.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new test files or additions to existing tests. Verify edge cases and error paths are covered.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new parser has no tests. Could we add tests covering valid input, malformed input, and empty input?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every behavioral change should have a corresponding test.

items.text: Are includes minimal and forward declarations used where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Headers should include only what they need. Use forward declarations in headers and include the full definition in .cpp files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary includes increase compile times and create coupling. A change to an included header forces recompilation of everything that includes it.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new headers for includes that could be forward declarations. Look for unnecessary transitive includes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This header includes <vector> and <string> but only uses pointers to those types. Forward declare them instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include what you use, but prefer forward declarations in headers.

items.text: Does the code compile without warnings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code should compile cleanly with -Wall -Wextra. Warnings often indicate real bugs like signed/unsigned comparison or unused variables.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ignoring warnings normalizes potential bugs. Many warnings (implicit conversions, missing returns) indicate undefined behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI output for new warnings. Test locally with -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic.
baseContent.exampleComment: This comparison between signed and unsigned integers triggers a warning. Use explicit casts or matching types.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Treat warnings as errors. Fix them, do not suppress them without good reason.
